question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: isNew()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a crafted record-stream replay that causes isNew() to always return true, bypassing Hibernate's pre-insert existence check, so a duplicate consensusTimestamp row is force-inserted, silently overwriting or conflicting with the canonical Hashgraph history entry for that timestamp?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an Ethereum transaction whose hash field is null or zero-length, causing toContractTransactionHash() to propagate a null/empty hash into the contract_transaction_hash table, making the canonical transaction permanently unlookupable by hash and corrupting Hashgraph history integrity?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a transaction where payerAccountId is null at the time toContractTransactionHash() is called, triggering a NullPointerException on payerAccountId.getId() that aborts the persistence pipeline and leaves the contract_transaction_hash table missing the entry for that consensus timestamp, creating a gap in Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: setBloom()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a contract transaction whose bloom filter is exactly 256 zero bytes (matching EMPTY_BLOOM), causing setBloom() to replace it with ArrayUtils.EMPTY_BYTE_ARRAY, so that downstream bloom-based log filtering returns incorrect results and misrepresents the historical execution record of that contract call?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getId()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a consensusTimestamp collision by submitting transactions that produce two ContractResult records with the same consensusTimestamp value, where getId() returns the same Long for both, causing one record to silently shadow the other in the database and permanently falsifying the historical execution record?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with a transactionHash shorter than 32 bytes, causing toContractTransactionHash() to store a truncated hash as the @Id in ContractTransactionHash, making the record unreachable via the standard 32-byte hash lookup and effectively erasing that transaction from the queryable Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a transaction whose transactionHash is a 32-byte all-zeros value, causing toContractTransactionHash() to insert a zero-hash entry into contract_transaction_hash, which then collides with or shadows other zero-hash entries and corrupts hash-based historical lookups for all affected transactions?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: isNew()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the isNew()=true invariant to cause a primary key constraint violation on contract_result (consensus_timestamp, contract_id) by triggering two ContractResult inserts for the same timestamp, causing the importer to crash or skip subsequent records and creating a permanent gap in the mirrored Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a failed Ethereum transaction where contractId defaults to 0 (as set in addDefaultEthereumTransactionContractResult), causing toContractTransactionHash() to store entityId=0 in contract_transaction_hash, making the record appear to belong to a non-existent contract and corrupting the historical contract-to-transaction mapping?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: setBloom()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a contract execution that produces a bloom filter of exactly 256 bytes all set to zero, causing setBloom() to store an empty byte array instead, so that a verifier comparing the stored bloom against the original Hashgraph record detects a mismatch and cannot confirm the integrity of the historical execution?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit multiple Ethereum transactions with the same transactionHash value (hash collision or replay), causing toContractTransactionHash() to attempt inserting duplicate @Id byte[] values into contract_transaction_hash, where the isNew()=true pattern skips conflict detection and results in a DB error or silent data corruption of the historical hash index?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that transactionResult is stored as Integer (nullable) in ContractResult but as Integer in ContractTransactionHash, by triggering a null transactionResult scenario, causing toContractTransactionHash() to store a null transaction_result that breaks the success-priority ordering query used by getContractTransactionDetailsByHash() and returns wrong historical data?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: isNew()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a record-stream re-ingestion scenario where isNew() always returns true, causing Hibernate to attempt re-inserting already-persisted ContractResult records, resulting in constraint violations that halt the importer and prevent new Hashgraph history from being mirrored?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a contract transaction where transactionHash is longer than 32 bytes, causing toContractTransactionHash() to store an oversized hash as the @Id, which then fails to match any 32-byte lookup from the REST API and makes the transaction permanently invisible in the historical record?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getId()] Can an unprivileged external user cause consensusTimestamp to be set to Long.MIN_VALUE or Long.MAX_VALUE through a crafted record item, causing getId() to return an extreme value that breaks partition range queries on the contract_result table and makes all historical records in that partition inaccessible?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction where the transactionHash field in ContractResult differs from the hash stored in the ethereum_transaction table for the same consensusTimestamp, causing toContractTransactionHash() to persist a mismatched hash that makes the REST API return a different transaction than the one actually recorded in Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: setBloom()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a contract execution with a bloom filter of exactly 256 bytes where all bytes are zero except one, causing setBloom() to store the full 256-byte bloom (not normalized), while a different execution with all-zero bloom is normalized to empty, creating an inconsistent bloom representation across historical records that breaks log filter queries?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that contractId is a primitive long (not nullable) in ContractResult but entityId is a long in ContractTransactionHash, by triggering a scenario where contractId=0 is stored, causing the hash-to-contract mapping to point to entity 0 and misrepresent which contract executed the historical transaction?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: isNew()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the isNew()=true design (which skips SELECT-before-INSERT to avoid Hibernate querying) to cause a race condition where two concurrent importer threads both attempt to insert a ContractResult with the same consensusTimestamp, resulting in one thread's data being lost and the historical record being incomplete?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction where transactionHash is set to a value that is a prefix-collision with another transaction's hash (first 32 bytes identical), causing toContractTransactionHash() to store a hash that resolves to the wrong transaction when queried via the REST API, effectively swapping two transactions in the historical record?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: setBloom()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a contract execution where the bloom byte array is null (not set), causing setBloom() to be bypassed entirely and bloom stored as null in the database, while the REST API's ContractResultViewModel encodes null bloom as '0x', misrepresenting the historical execution as having an empty bloom when the actual Hashgraph record may differ?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a failed Ethereum transaction that triggers addDefaultEthereumTransactionContractResult() with contractId=0, then call toContractTransactionHash() which stores entityId=0, causing the /api/v1/contracts/results/{hash} endpoint to return a result associated with entity 0 instead of the actual contract, falsifying the historical execution record?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: isNew()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where the importer processes a record file containing a ContractResult whose consensusTimestamp matches an existing record, and because isNew() always returns true, the INSERT fails with a constraint violation that is swallowed by the batch processor, causing the new record to be silently dropped and the historical record to remain stale?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: toContractTransactionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction where transactionHash in ContractResult is the Hedera network hash (first 32 bytes of the Hedera transaction hash) rather than the Ethereum hash, causing toContractTransactionHash() to store the wrong hash type, making the transaction unreachable via Ethereum hash lookup and misrepresenting the historical record for EVM-compatible clients?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/contract/ContractResult.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getId()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction that causes consensusTimestamp to be set to 0 or negative, causing getId() to return 0/negative, which falls outside the valid partition range for contract_result and causes the record to be inserted into the wrong partition or rejected, creating a gap in the Hashgraph history?
